cards.text: A {{c1::default constructor}} is a constructor that can be called with {{c2::no arguments}}. It either has no parameters or all parameters have default values.
If you don't provide any constructors, the compiler will generate a {{c1::implicit default constructor}} that {{c2::default-initializes}} all members.

cards.front: What happens when you define any constructor for a class?
cards.back: The compiler will NOT generate an implicit default constructor. If you want a default constructor, you must explicitly define one yourself.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: No default constructor available. Once you define any constructor, the implicit default constructor is not generated.
cards.explanation: Foo has a constructor that requires an int parameter. The implicit default constructor is gone, so Foo f; doesn't work. Use Foo f{5}; or add an explicit default constructor.

cards.text:
To explicitly define a default constructor:
{{c1::ClassName()}} {{c2::= default;}}  // compiler-generated
or
{{c3::ClassName()}} { } // user-defined

cards.concept: Using default arguments to create a default constructor
cards.explanation: You can provide default values for all constructor parameters, making it callable with no arguments. This serves as both a parameterized constructor and a default constructor.
examples: Fraction(int num = 0, int den = 1) : m_num{num}, m_den{den} { }

cards.front: What is the difference between = default and an empty constructor body?
cards.back: = default generates a compiler-provided default constructor. An empty body { } is user-provided. For most purposes they're similar, but = default may be more optimizable and works better with certain language features.

cards.question: How many ways can you instantiate objects from this class?
cards.code:
class Point
{
public:
    Point(int x = 0, int y = 0) : m_x{x}, m_y{y} { }
private:
    int m_x, m_y;
};
cards.answer:
Point p1;
Point p2{5};
Point p3{5, 10};
All three work due to default arguments.
cards.explanation: Default arguments make the constructor callable with 0, 1, or 2 arguments, providing flexibility.

cards.text: Best practice: If you define any constructors for a class, consider whether the class should also have a {{c1::default constructor}}. If so, {{c2::provide one}}.

cards.front: Can a class have both a default constructor and other constructors?
cards.back: Yes. You can have a default constructor (no parameters) and other constructors with parameters. This is constructor overloading.

cards.question: What's wrong with this instantiation?
cards.answer: Most vexing parse! Widget w(); declares a function, not a variable. Use Widget w; or Widget w{}; instead.
cards.explanation: C++ interprets Widget w(); as a function declaration that takes no parameters and returns a Widget. This is a famous gotcha.

cards.front: When should you use = default for the default constructor?
cards.back: Use = default when you want the compiler-generated behavior and don't need to add custom initialization logic. It's clearer intent and may enable certain optimizations.
